Chromatin extracts were precleared for 2 h at 4°C with 20 μL of Dynabeads M-280 sheep anti-rabbit IgG (11203D, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). 4 μg of MeCP2 antibody (M9317, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA), 4 μg histone H3 antibody (ab1791, Abcam, Cambridge, UK), and 4 μg of normal IgG control antibody (I5006, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) were coupled for 2 h at 4°C with 20 μL of Dynabeads. Precleared extracts were incubated with the antibody (Ab)-bead complexes overnight at 4°C. The recovered material was treated with RNAse, DNAse-free (11119915001, Merck Millipore, Burlington, MA, USA) and reverse cross-linked with 0.2 mg/mL of Proteinase K overnight at 65°C, followed by phenol/chloroform extraction and ethanol precipitation. Immunoprecipitated DNA samples (MeCP2, IgG, input, and histone H3, the two former two with 1:10 dilution) were analyzed by qRT-PCR using SYBR Green Master Mix (Applied Biosystems, Foster City, CA, USA) in the QuantStudio 5 real-time PCR system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The primers used are shown in Table S1.

sec.title: RNA ISH
sec.p: ReNCells VM were seeded on coverslips pre-treated with laminin. Following two washes with PBS (5 mM CaCl2 and 5 mM MgCl2), cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min and washed twice with PBS. RNA was hybridized with the hsa_circ_0050946 probe (VPTZ76X, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) using the ViewRNA miRNA ISH cell assay (QVCM0001, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) in a HybEZ II oven (Advanced Cell Diagnostic, San Francisco, CA, USA) according to the manufacturer’s recommendations. Coverslips were blocked with blocking solution (5% chicken serum and 0.1% Triton X-100 in PBS) for 1 h at RT. Coverslips were then incubated with the neural protein TUJ1 Ab (1:500; T8660, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) in blocking solution overnight at 4°C in the dark and covered with wet tissues. The next day, coverslips were washed three times with PBS and incubated with the secondary Ab Alexa Fluor 488 chicken anti-mouse (1:1,000; A-21200, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) in blocking solution for 45 min at RT. After three washes with PBS, coverslips were incubated with DAPI (1:10,000) for 5 min at RT before mounting on microscope slides with FluorSave reagent (345789, Merck Millipore, Burlington, MA, USA), dried overnight at RT in the dark, and finally stored at 4°C. Images of hybridized cells were collected using an Axio Observer Z1 fluorescence microscope with ZEN blue 2012 software (Zeiss, Jena, Germany) and analyzed with ImageJ v.1.51h.

sec.title: Characterization of mutations of individuals with RTT
sec.p: Genomic DNA was extracted from frozen cerebral tissue using the saline-urea method as follows. Frozen tissue was washed with saline buffer (2 M Tris and MgCl2), and the pellet was rescued after centrifugation and washed with urea buffer (urea, 5 M NaCl, 0.5 M EDTA, 2 M Tris, and 10% SDS). Samples were then incubated for 2 h at 56°C and subjected to phenol-chloroform extraction and precipitation with 3 M sodium acetate and ethanol. Pellets were resuspended with TE buffer. All DNA samples were quantified using a Qubit 2.0 fluorometer (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA, USA). DNA purity was quantified by calculating the absorbance ratio (A260/A280) with a NanoDrop 1000 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The DNA was also Sanger sequenced to confirm MeCP2 mutations in RTT samples and to exclude mutations in control samples, as described elsewhere.110 A list of the primers used for sequencing can be found in Table S1.

sec.title: Whole-proteome analysis
sec.p: Four biopsies from human healthy HIP and HIP from individuals with RTT were processed, digested, and analyzed in an Orbitrap Fusion Lumos (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) at the CRG Proteomics Core Facility (Barcelona, Spain) following standard procedures. Raw data were analyzed with MaxQuant software v.1.6.2.6a. The statistical analysis was performed with the Limma package in R, which was used to implement a linear model. Proteins quantified in at least three of four samples of each condition (control and RTT) were analyzed further. 51 significantly regulated proteins (Benjamini-Hochberg-adjusted p < 0.05) were obtained.

sec.title: Editing and alternative splicing analysis
sec.p: To identify the selected exon and the editing levels in GRIA3, the region surrounding the interrogated edited position (including the alternative exon) was amplified with specific primers by using 250 ng of cDNA from each sample and Immolase DNA polymerase (BIO21047, Meridian Bioscience, Cincinnati, OH, USA) for 35 cycles. The resulting PCR products were gel purified with NucleoSpin Gel and PCR Clean-up (740609.250, Macherey-Nagel, Düren, Germany) and cloned into the pGEM-T Easy Vector (A1360, Promega, Madison, WI, USA) according to the manufacturer’s protocol. The GRIA3 insert of each colony was amplified with Immolase DNA polymerase (BIO21047, Meridian Bioscience, Cincinnati, OH, USA) for 35 cycles using specific pGEM-T primers and precipitated with 87.7% of absolute ethanol and 12.3% of 3 M NaAc. The precipitated PCR product was sequenced using the T7 primer 5′TAATACGACTCACTATAGGG′3 in the 3730 DNA Analyzer (Applied Biosystems, Foster City, CA, USA) and analyzed with BioEdit v.7.2.5 software. For the postmortem samples, results represent the pool of sequences from three controls and three individuals with RTT. After sequencing analysis, the percentages of A or G and the corresponding exons were calculated and analyzed with Fisher’s exact test.

sec.title: DNA methylation analysis
sec.p: The methylation state of GRIA3 alternative exons was determined by bisulfite genomic sequencing (BSP). The Methyl Primer Express v.1.0 software (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) was used to design specific primers to amplify the regions of interest (a list of oligos can be found in Table S1). Genomic DNA (1 μg) was subjected to sodium bisulfite treatment using the EZ DNA Methylation-Gold Kit (D5006, Zymo Research, Irvine, CA, USA), which induces chemical conversion of unmethylated, but not methylated, cytosine to uracil. Fragments of 200- to 300-bp pairs were amplified using 1.2 μL of bisulfite-converted DNA with Immolase DNA polymerase (BIO21047, Meridian Bioscience, Cincinnati, OH, USA) in a final volume of 15 μL for 40 cycles. The resulting PCR products were gel purified with NucleoSpin Gel and PCR Clean-up (740609.250, Macherey-Nagel, Düren, Germany) and then cloned into the pGEM-T Easy Vector (A1360, Promega, Madison, WI, USA) according to the manufacturer’s protocol. For all samples, a minimum of 20 clones were chosen randomly and sequenced using a 3730 DNA Analyzer (Applied Biosystems, Foster City, CA, USA). Results were analyzed with BioEdit v.7.2.5 software, and methylated cytosines were mapped. Cytosine nucleotides that remained unaltered were transformed into percentages of CpGs showing methylation.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: All experiments were performed at least in triplicate. Graphs and statistical analyses were obtained with GraphPad Prism 8.2.0. Unless otherwise stated, qRT-PCR data are expressed as the median with interquartile range. Comparative analyses of differences between experimental groups were performed using Mann-Whitney U test or unpaired-sample t tests and one-way ANOVA with a Tukey or Bonferroni post hoc test for intergroup comparisons. Results were considered significant for values of p < 0.05, p < 0.01, or p < 0.001 (∗∗∗). Gene enrichment analyses from significantly altered genes were conducted using Enrichr v.2.0 software (http://amp.pharm.mssm.edu/Enrichr/)111,112 and https://reactome.org/.113

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: We thank the CERCA program (Generalitat de Catalunya) and the Josep Carreras Foundation for institutional support, Dr. Vaquero (IJC, Badalona, Spain) for the generous gift of the SIRT2-HA plasmid, and members of the Pasterkamp lab for discussions and help. This work was supported by the 10.13039/501100003329Ministerio de Economía y Competitividad and the 10.13039/501100004587Instituto de Salud Carlos III, co-financed by the European Development Regional Fund, “A way to achieve Europe” ERDF, under grants PI15/01159 and PI20/00389 (to J.A.), the crowd-funding program PRECIPITA, FondoBiorett and Mi Princesa Rett (to J.A.), the Health and Science Departments of the Catalan Government (Generalitat de Catalunya), the Catalan and Spanish Associations for Rett Syndrome (FinRett, to S.G.), the 10.13039/501100006117Epilepsiefonds (WAR18-05, to R.J.P.), and the European Union Horizon 2020 Research and Innovation Program under Marie Skłodowska-Curie grant agreement 721890 (cirRTrain ITN, to R.J.P.). We are indebted to the “Biobanc de l’Hospital Infantil Sant Joan de Déu per a la Investigació,” integrated in the Spanish Biobank Network of ISCIII, for sample and data procurement. G.A.C. is the Felix L. Haas Endowed Professor in Basic Science. G.A.C.’s laboratory is supported by National Institutes of Health (NIH/NCATS) grant UH3TR00943-01 through the NIH Common Fund, Office of Strategic Coordination (OSC); NCI grants 1R01 CA182905-01 and 1R01CA222007-01A1; NIGMS 1R01GM122775-01; a Team DOD grant (CA160445P1); a Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia Moonshot Flagship project; a CLL Global Research Foundation 2019 grant; a CLL Global Research Foundation 2020 grant; donor support through Dr. Jaffer Ajani; and the Estate of C.G. Johnson, Jr. A.O.-G. is a pre-doctoral fellow funded by MINECO (BES-2015-071452) and the recipient of an EMBO short-term fellowship (8129). E.S. is a pre-doctoral fellow funded by a “Science without borders” scholarship (202074/2015-3) from Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq, Brasilia, Brazil). M.E. is an ICREA Research Professor.

sec.title: Author contributions
sec.p: All experiments were conceived by E.S., A.O.-G., O.C.J.-T., and S.G. and carried out mainly by E.S. and A.O.-G. O.C.J.-T. carried out the proteomics analysis and experiments with the N2a model. M.S. performed the methylation analysis and provided technical support. F.S. carried out plasmid construction. C.O.-M. and D.R.-K. performed circRNA characterization. A.P.-A., C.X., and J.A. genotyped the RTT samples. G.A.C., M.S., and C.I. performed T-UCR microarray hybridization and analysis. D.v.R. and R.J.P. provided training and assisted with RNA ISH experiments. S.G. wrote the manuscript with input from all authors. S.G. and M.E. supervised the project.
